summary:
The Fall of Granada (January 2, 1492 AD) was the culminating event of the seven-century [[event:2209|Reconquista]], as the Catholic Monarchs Ferdinand II of Aragon and Isabella I of Castile captured the Emirate of Granada — the last Muslim state on the Iberian Peninsula — ending Islamic political authority in Spain after 781 years. The campaign had been building for a decade; the Emirate of Granada, weakened by internal dynastic conflicts (the civil war between the Nasrid claimants Boabdil and his father and uncle), finally agreed to surrender terms after a siege of the capital city.

The surrender was negotiated rather than conquered by storm, and the terms initially promised religious freedom and protection of property to the Muslim population. Boabdil (Muhammad XII), the last Nasrid sultan, handed over the keys of the Alhambra palace — one of the greatest architectural achievements of medieval Islamic civilization — and reportedly wept as he looked back at the city from a mountain pass, earning the location the name 'Pass of the Moor's Sigh.' Within years, the promises of religious tolerance were abandoned: the [[event:2287|Spanish Inquisition]] increased its activities, Archbishop Cisneros ordered mass baptisms and burned Arabic manuscripts in Granada's main square, and by 1502 Muslims were ordered to convert or leave. The same year as the fall of Granada, Columbus sailed west, and the Jews were expelled under the Alhambra Decree — making 1492 the most consequential year in Spanish history, marking both the completion of medieval Christian Spain and the opening of Spain's global empire.
